metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which batch step (round_a, round_b, or a new dedicated step) receives the project-criteria fan-in, and describe the deduplication mechanism (analogous to plan-review's `exclude` set) that guarantees exactly-once execution across the two-round topology.
evidence: 'A synthetic activated code-review project.* criterion runs exactly once in Pass 1'
code-review.yaml has two separate batch steps: round_a (lines 77-94) and round_b (lines 109-127), each independently constructing a CodeReviewBatchRunner
ProductionBatchRunner._project_criteria uses an `exclude` set to deduplicate — no equivalent deduplication strategy is specified for the two-runner code-review topology
findings.finding: The plan does not specify which of the two batch steps (round_a or round_b) receives the project-criteria fan-in, nor how double-execution across both rounds is prevented. The YAML runs two independent `CodeReviewBatchRunner` instances (round_a and round_b). If the fan-in appends project criteria to both, a project criterion executes twice — violating the plan's 'exactly once' guarantee. The plan-review analog (`_project_criteria`) appends to a single batch request inside `ProductionBatchRunner.run()`; code review's two-round structure makes the insertion point non-obvious and the exclusion logic is unspecified.
findings.location: Scope section ('runs exactly once in Pass 1') / src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/code-review.yaml:round_a and round_b steps / src/rebar/llm/code_review/batch_runner.py
scenarios: Fan-in is added to CodeReviewBatchRunner.run() unconditionally; both round_a and round_b BatchRunRequests receive the project criterion and it executes twice, producing duplicate findings that inflate Pass-2 budget and skew Pass-3 decisions.
Fan-in is added only to round_b, but there is no guarantee a project criterion doesn't also appear in round_a via a future YAML change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify where the `code-review-project-<name>` forward-mapping function lives (e.g. a new `CODE_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX` constant + gate-parameterized overload in `criteria/ids.py`) and confirm whether the criterion authoring path (`workflow/criterion_preview.py`, `editor_server.py`) is out-of-scope or needs a guard to use the correct prefix per gate.
evidence: Plan: 'Map a logical project.<name> criterion to physical prompt id code-review-project-<name-with-dots-replaced-by-dashes>; Routing JSON continues to use the logical id as its key; translation occurs only when prompt/eval assets are dispatched.'
ids.py line 31: PLAN_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX = 'plan-review-' — the only prefix constant; criterion_prompt_id() returns f'{PLAN_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX}{criterion_id.replace(".", "-")}' unconditionally

criterion_preview.py calls criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id) for the authoring path — would produce 'plan-review-project-review-phase-boundaries' instead of the code-review physical id for a code-review project criterion author
Plan does not mention updating ids.py, criterion_preview.py, or the editor to handle the second gate prefix
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new 'code-review-project-<name>' physical prompt-ID prefix but does not specify where the forward-mapping function lives — whether criteria/ids.py gains a gate_key-parameterized overload or a new CODE_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX constant, or the mapping is inlined only in batch_runner/eval_solver. The existing criterion_prompt_id() is hardcoded to 'plan-review-' and is called from the criterion authoring path (workflow/criterion_preview.py, editor_server.py), which would silently produce the wrong physical ID for a code-review project criterion author using the same tooling.
findings.location: Scope section ('Map a logical project.<name> criterion to physical prompt id code-review-project-<name-with-dots-replaced-by-dashes>') and src/rebar/llm/criteria/ids.py
scenarios: A developer uses 'rebar criteria' authoring tooling to scaffold a code-review project criterion; criterion_prompt_id() generates 'plan-review-project-review-phase-boundaries' instead of 'code-review-project-review-phase-boundaries', creating a mismatched prompt file that the batch runner cannot find.
The mapping is inlined in two places (batch_runner and eval_solver) rather than in the shared ids.py module, causing future drift when a third consumer needs it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `CODE_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX = 'code-review-'` to `criteria/ids.py` and a gate-dispatched `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id, *, gate='plan_review') -> str` overload. The authoring path is already out-of-scope for this story (scope section excludes 'authoring project.review-phase-boundaries'), so add a one-line scope clarification: the authoring path (criterion_preview/editor) is left for a follow-on story and a code-review project criterion author must currently create the physical prompt file manually.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a processing protocol to the plan for the `criteria eval` CLI preflight: specify whether to check plan-review registry first then code-review `effective_criteria`, or to dispatch on gate membership, and define what error message is shown when an id appears in neither.
evidence: _llm_eval_commands.py lines 145-155: preflight checks only `registry.by_id(repo_root)` (plan-review registry); a code-review project id is not in that registry and is rejected.

Plan Scope: 'Extend `criteria eval` preflight to accept a logical id only when it is active in code review's repository-effective criteria; continue to reject unknown or inactive ids before solver dispatch.'
The plan never specifies a decision rule for distinguishing plan-review vs code-review project ids at the CLI level (both use the `project.<name>` namespace).
findings.finding: `_criteria_eval` in `_llm_eval_commands.py` hard-codes `plan_review.registry.by_id(repo_root)` as its only preflight gate. The plan requires this to also accept activated code-review project logical ids, but gives no protocol: which registry to check for code-review ids, in which order relative to the plan-review check, or how to distinguish a plan-review `project.*` id from a code-review `project.*` id.
findings.location: AC line 'Extend `criteria eval` preflight to accept a logical id only when it is active in code review's repository-effective criteria' / src/rebar/_cli/_llm_eval_commands.py lines 143-155
scenarios: `rebar criteria eval project.review-phase-boundaries` fails with 'unknown criterion' even after the bug is fixed, because the preflight only checks the plan-review registry.

An implementer may add a fallback to `code_review.effective_criteria` for any id not in the plan-review registry, but without a spec may also check both registries in parallel or require an explicit gate flag.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the preflight to fall back to `code_review.effective_criteria(repo_root)` when the id is not in `plan_review.registry.by_id(repo_root)`, rejecting with the existing message only when it appears in neither.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `code_review_decide` obtains repository-effective routing: describe whether `effective_routing(ctx.repo_root)` is called inside the step (using `ctx.repo_root` already available to `StepContext`) or passed as a computed input, and update both `registry.threshold_for` and `registry.nit_suppressed_criteria` call sites to receive that map.
evidence: 'Pass 3 applies repository-effective threshold, blocking posture, and nit-suppression routing for the synthetic id without invoking an LLM'
code_review_decide calls `registry.threshold_for` (line 419) and `registry.nit_suppressed_criteria()` (line 427) with no routing_map arg — both default to packaged routing_index()
The decide step YAML wires no repo_root input; ctx.repo_root is set by the executor from the workflow-run context, but the step currently does not use it for routing resolution
Both registry functions already accept an optional routing_map parameter, but the wiring from ctx.repo_root → effective_routing(ctx.repo_root) → routing_map is absent
findings.finding: Pass-3 routing wiring is under-specified: `code_review_decide` currently calls `registry.threshold_for` and `registry.nit_suppressed_criteria()` without a `routing_map` argument, so they silently fall back to the packaged `routing_index()`. The plan says Pass 3 must use repository-effective routing, but it does not describe how `ctx.repo_root` reaches `code_review_decide` (the step has no `repo_root` YAML input today) nor how the effective routing map is threaded into the two registry calls inside the step. Without this wiring, a project criterion's configured `block_threshold`, `blocking_enabled`, or `nit_suppressed` would be ignored in Pass 3 even after the fan-in lands — silently breaking the story's core posture contract.
scenarios: A project activates `project.review-phase-boundaries` with `block_threshold: 0.90` and `blocking_enabled: false`; Pass 3 resolves it against the packaged index (no entry → default 0.95, blocking=false) — the configured 0.90 threshold is silently ignored.
A project overlay sets `nit_suppressed: true` on a built-in criterion to reduce noise; Pass 3 still reads the packaged index and the suppression never takes effect.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly in the plan that both `registry.threshold_for` and `registry.nit_suppressed_criteria` calls in `code_review_decide` must be passed `routing_map=registry.effective_routing(ctx.repo_root)`, naming the call sites in `workflow_ops.py` lines 416-427.
evidence: workflow_ops.py line 419: `threshold_for=registry.threshold_for` (uses default routing_map=None → packaged routing_index)
workflow_ops.py line 427: `nit_suppressed = registry.nit_suppressed_criteria()` (no routing_map → packaged routing_index)
Plan Scope: 'Bind Pass 3 threshold and nit-suppression lookup to `effective_routing(ctx.repo_root)`'

`StepContext` has `.repo_root`; `registry.threshold_for` and `registry.nit_suppressed_criteria` both accept an optional `routing_map` parameter — the wiring is mechanically straightforward but not specified as a processing protocol in the plan.
The plan names `workflow_ops.py` as a seam but does not name the specific functions or lines to update.
findings.finding: `code_review_decide` calls `registry.threshold_for` and `registry.nit_suppressed_criteria()` with no `routing_map` argument (defaulting to the packaged `routing_index()`). The plan says to 'bind Pass-3 threshold and nit-suppression lookup to `effective_routing(ctx.repo_root)`', but the plan never specifies which call site or how `ctx.repo_root` is threaded through; it names only `workflow_ops.py` as the seam file without identifying the specific lines.
findings.location: Scope section ('Pass 3 applies repository-effective threshold, blocking posture, and nit-suppression routing') / src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py lines 416-427
scenarios: An implementer may wire `effective_routing(ctx.repo_root)` into only one of the two calls (threshold_for or nit_suppressed_criteria), leaving the other bound to packaged routing — the project overlay's nit-suppression or threshold re-tunes would silently not apply.
Partial wiring produces a mixed state where blocking posture uses project routing but nit-suppression does not.
findings.suggested_fix: In `code_review_decide`, add `_routing = registry.effective_routing(ctx.repo_root)` before line 416, then pass `threshold_for=lambda c, rm=_routing: registry.threshold_for(c, rm)` and `registry.nit_suppressed_criteria(_routing)` respectively.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit named beneficiary (e.g., 'criterion authors configuring project-specific code-review rules') and a one-sentence description of their current broken workflow to the Why section.
evidence: 'A project can therefore configure a net-new LLM-backed code-review criterion that merges successfully yet never runs end to end.' — no human role named
'Downstream criterion authoring depends on this story.' — beneficiary inferable but never stated explicitly
findings.finding: The plan never explicitly names its beneficiary — the stakeholder harmed by the defect is described only in system terms ('a project can configure a criterion that never runs'), with 'criterion authors' or 'project teams' inferable only from 'Downstream criterion authoring depends on this story'. F4(a) requires naming a specific user/stakeholder and the problem they face.
findings.location: Why section; Sequencing and Rollback section
scenarios: A plan reviewer cannot confirm the value story without knowing who is blocked and how their workflow is impaired; the absence makes it harder to write an acceptance scenario from the user's perspective
findings.suggested_fix: Prepend to the Why section: 'Criterion authors who configure project-specific code-review rules today see their overlay merge successfully but produce no review output — a silent failure that is indistinguishable from a missing overlay.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State in the Scope section which mechanism implements the physical-to-logical inverse lookup (e.g. 'iterate the active criteria map and return the entry whose physical id equals the input; reject if none match') so the executor has a single unambiguous strategy.
evidence: 'logical inverse' is the only description of the reverse-mapping strategy
The mapping rule is one-way as stated: logical → physical; the reverse is implied but not specified
No mention of whether a registry, a regex strip, or a bidirectional dict should be used
findings.finding: The phrase 'recognize the sanitized physical id only when its logical inverse is active' does not specify the mechanism for computing the inverse mapping at runtime — an executor could implement a regex-based string reversal, a registry lookup, or a bidirectional dict, and each has different edge-case behavior (e.g. a physical id matching multiple logical ids, or a physical id that happens to match the pattern but isn't registered).
findings.location: Scope section: 'Extend `_code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id, repo_root)` to recognize the sanitized physical id only when its logical inverse is active'
scenarios: Executor implements regex-based reversal that strips the `code-review-` prefix and replaces dashes with dots, producing a logical id that was never registered, leading to silent acceptance of malformed physical ids
Executor builds a bidirectional registry but the plan gives no guidance on where it lives, causing architectural divergence from plan-review's fan-in pattern
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'The inverse lookup iterates the repository-effective criteria map, finds the entry whose computed physical id equals the input physical id, and returns the logical id; if no entry matches, the id is rejected as unknown.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Promote the `project.<name>` → `code-review-project-<name-with-dots-replaced-by-dashes>` translation rule to a named, tested constant (or a one-entry registry) and explicitly document it as a stable authoring contract in `docs/llm-framework.md`, including which characters are valid in criterion names.
evidence: 'Map a logical `project.<name>` criterion to physical prompt id `code-review-project-<name-with-dots-replaced-by-dashes>`; for this epic the physical prompt/eval basename is `code-review-project-review-phase-boundaries`. Routing JSON continues to use the logical id as its key; translation occurs only when prompt/eval assets are dispatched.'

ABSENCE: no mention of placing the translation rule behind a named, versioned constant, registry, or config entry; no explicit statement that `docs/llm-framework.md` will document the naming convention as a stable contract for criterion authors.
The rule is asserted (dots replaced by dashes) rather than derived, and future criterion names may contain characters not covered by this rule (e.g., underscores, numbers as leading characters).
findings.finding: The physical prompt-id naming convention (`project.<name>` → `code-review-project-<name-with-dots-replaced-by-dashes>`) is a hardcoded translation rule inside `_code_review_prompt_id` that becomes an implicit contract every future project-criterion author must honor, yet it is neither configurable nor explicitly documented as a stable, versioned interface in the planned doc update.
findings.location: Scope section — 'Map a logical `project.<name>` criterion to physical prompt id `code-review-project-<name-with-dots-replaced-by-dashes>`'; also implicitly in AC2 and AC5.
scenarios: A new project criterion named `project.check_security` (underscore) silently produces an unexpected physical id or breaks asset resolution because the rule only specifies dot-to-dash substitution.

The naming convention changes in a future story; all existing criterion-to-asset mappings break silently because the rule is embedded in a single function rather than a declared, discoverable contract.
A criterion author reads `docs/llm-framework.md` but cannot find the naming-convention contract and invents a non-conforming basename.
findings.suggested_fix: Define a module-level constant `PROJECT_CRITERION_PROMPT_PREFIX = 'code-review-project-'` and a pure function `logical_to_physical_prompt_id(logical_id: str) -> str` that encodes the substitution rule (and raises on invalid characters). Reference this function from `_code_review_prompt_id` and from the `criteria eval` preflight. Add a one-liner to `docs/llm-framework.md` specifying the naming convention and valid character set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence affirmative rationale for the batch-runner fan-in approach (e.g. 'chosen because it reuses the established `ProductionBatchRunner` pattern, requires no workflow DSL schema change, and preserves the interpreter's per-criterion when/probe gating intact').
evidence: 'Reuse plan review's runner-side dynamic fan-in pattern without adding a workflow DSL field or a hard-coded project criterion id' — states only what is NOT done, not why this approach is preferred

No sentence explains the positive case: e.g. that batch-runner fan-in is chosen because it is the established pattern (ProductionBatchRunner), requires no workflow schema change, and preserves the interpreter's per-criterion when/probe gating
findings.finding: The plan states a negative rationale ('without adding a workflow DSL field or a hard-coded project criterion id') but omits an affirmative statement explaining why the batch-runner fan-in approach is the right fit for this codebase. An implementer who hasn't studied `ProductionBatchRunner` cannot tell from the plan text why this approach was chosen over e.g. prepending project criteria to the YAML batch lists at dispatch time (in `_assemble_code_review_run`) or adding an overlay-aware trigger step. Absence of positive rationale is a G6 MINOR finding.
scenarios: An implementer unfamiliar with the codebase chooses the dispatch-time approach (mutating the gate doc before workflow execution) because the plan doesn't explain why that was rejected, leading to a workflow-schema-visible change that breaks the schema-immutability constraint.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'The batch-runner fan-in is chosen because it reuses the same seam `ProductionBatchRunner` already provides, requires no change to the immutable workflow DSL schema, and keeps project criteria invisible to the interpreter's when-expression filter (which only applies to static YAML entries) — matching the plan-review precedent exactly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the gate-aware prompt-id mapping protocol in `ids.py`: add a `CODE_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX = 'code-review-'` constant and specify a gate-parameterised function (e.g. `criterion_prompt_id(cid, gate='plan_review')`) or a dedicated `code_review_criterion_prompt_id(cid)` function, and update `calibrate_criterion` to use the correct mapping for code-review project criteria.
evidence: ids.py line 36-42: `criterion_prompt_id` unconditionally returns `f"{PLAN_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX}{criterion_id.replace('.', '-')}"`
Plan Scope: 'Map a logical project.<name> criterion to physical prompt id code-review-project-<name-with-dots-replaced-by-dashes>'
Plan Scope: 'Reuse plan review's runner-side dynamic fan-in pattern without adding a workflow DSL field'
No `CODE_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX` constant exists anywhere in src/; no gate-parameterised overload of `criterion_prompt_id` is present or specified.
`calibrate_criterion` in eval.py calls `criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id)` unconditionally (line 419); a code-review project criterion would receive the wrong physical prompt id.
findings.finding: `criterion_prompt_id()` in `ids.py` unconditionally emits a `plan-review-` prefix for ALL gates, but the plan requires a gate-aware mapping that emits `code-review-project-<name>` for code-review project criteria. No `CODE_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX` constant, no gate-parameterised variant, and no dispatch protocol is defined anywhere in the plan text or the named seam file.
findings.location: Scope section ('Map a logical project.<name> criterion to physical prompt id code-review-project-<name-with-dots-replaced-by-dashes>') / src/rebar/llm/criteria/ids.py
scenarios: An implementer extending `ids.py` has no spec for the gate-dispatch protocol — they may add a second function, add a `gate=` parameter, or create a new constant, producing divergent implementations.

`calibrate_criterion` calls `criterion_prompt_id` without any gate context; a code-review project criterion's eval fixture would be looked up under `plan-review-project-<name>.eval.yaml` instead of `code-review-project-<name>.eval.yaml`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `CODE_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX = 'code-review-'` and `def code_review_criterion_prompt_id(criterion_id: str) -> str: return f"{CODE_REVIEW_PROMPT_PREFIX}{criterion_id.replace('.', '-')}"` to `ids.py`; update `calibrate_criterion` to dispatch on which gate's registry the criterion belongs to before choosing the prefix.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the complete updated signature of `_code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id, repo_root)` and the inversion algorithm (`code-review-project-<name>` → `project.<name>` via prefix-strip + prepend), and update the `run_case` call site at line 254 to pass `repo_root`.
evidence: Current `_code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id: str)` signature has no `repo_root` parameter.

Plan Scope: '`_code_review_prompt_id` therefore receives `repo_root`, maps physical `code-review-project-<name>` back to logical `project.<name>`, checks membership in `code_review.effective_criteria(repo_root)`, and returns the physical prompt id only on success.'

Plan Scope: 'Eval cases use the `diff` key and return the prompt's native `{findings: [...]}` output; they never run through the plan-review `_run_criterion_case` / `pass1_chunk` path and require no new solver arm.'

The plan says 'no new solver arm' but also says the function 'reuses the existing `_run_code_review_case` arm' — this creates ambiguity about whether the dispatch in `run_case` needs updating or not (the existing arm already calls `_run_code_review_case`; but `_code_review_prompt_id` currently rejects any non-OVERLAY_IDS id).
findings.finding: The plan requires `_code_review_prompt_id` in `eval_solver.py` to receive `repo_root` and perform an active-membership check via `code_review.effective_criteria(repo_root)`, but neither the updated function signature nor the inversion algorithm (physical `code-review-project-<name>` → logical `project.<name>`) is specified with enough detail to define a single unambiguous implementation; particularly, the plan says 'a prefix match alone is insufficient' but never specifies what the sufficient check is beyond a one-sentence description.
findings.location: Scope section ('Extend `_code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id, repo_root)` to recognize the sanitized physical id only when its logical inverse is active') / src/rebar/llm/evals/eval_solver.py lines 162-176
scenarios: An implementer does not know whether to add `repo_root` as a positional or keyword argument, or whether `run_case` passes it through; the plan says '`_code_review_prompt_id` therefore receives `repo_root`' but `run_case`'s call site at line 254 is `_code_review_prompt_id(prompt_id)` with no `repo_root`.
Without a defined inversion algorithm (e.g. strip `code-review-project-` prefix → prepend `project.`), two implementers may produce different logical-id recovery logic.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
